text: The surgical team begins the procedure with an inspection for metastases, followed by a laparoscopic ultrasound of the retroperitoneum. The dissection is initiated at the porta hepatis, proceeding from right to left to safely isolate the tumor interface with the portal vein.
The surgical field shows significant fibrosis due to treatment effects. The surgeon identifies the replaced common hepatic artery and notes its mobilization during further dissection.

The surgeon incises the inferior border of the pancreas, identifying the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and creating a retropancreatic tunnel. The common hepatic artery is retracted laterally, revealing the portal vein, which is further developed to complete the tunnel.

The surgeon transects the pancreas using a vessel sealer up to the point of tumor involvement with the portal vein, facilitating mobilization of the adherent portion. The splenic vein is controlled with a vessel loop and transected using a stapler.

The surgeon isolates the area where the pancreas adheres to the portal vein, placing a bulldog clamp across the portal vein without significantly narrowing it. The portal vein is then sharply transected and removed en bloc.
The surgeon repairs the vessel by over-sewing with a five-0 Prolene suture, ensuring the vessel's integrity. The dissection continues in the retroperitoneum, with lymph nodes being removed en bloc.

The surgeon retracts the stomach anteriorly to identify and dissect the celiac axis. The pancreas is retracted anteriorly and to the left, revealing the area of celiac involvement more clearly. Further dissection of the celiac axis is performed using this approach.

The surgeon dissects the celiac artery down to the level of the aorta, noting and further dissecting the proximal left gastric artery takeoff. An ICG angiogram is used to aid in the dissection and identification of the main celiac trunk, as well as the left gastric and splenic artery takeoffs.
The surgeon transects the celiac axis with a stapler, removing it en bloc just distal to the left gastric artery. The remaining retroperitoneal attachments to the pancreas are mobilized.
The surgeon completes gastric mobilization in both directions, followed by division of omental and splenic attachments, resulting in the specimen being completely free.

The specimen is placed in an endocatch bag and removed from the abdomen. The pancreatic margin is sent for frozen section analysis. Due to treatment effects, the frozen section is indeterminate for cancer, prompting the surgeon to take an additional margin in two pieces.
The surgeon performs additional retroperitoneal lymphadenectomy, carefully excising lymph nodes located in the aorto-caval groove.

The surgeon oversews the cut edge of the pancreas with a running proline suture to minimize post-operative leakage. A layer of Dermabond is applied and polymerized on top of the suture line for additional sealing.
